questionText: A, B and C can do a piece of work in 11 days, 20 days and 55 days respectively, working alone. How soon can the work be done if A is assisted by B and C on alternate days?

steps.explanation: On Day 1: A + B work together; on Day 2: A + C work together (alternate days)
steps.formulaExpression: (A+B) \text{ on odd days}, (A+C) \text{ on even days}

steps.explanation: After 3 cycles (6 days): work done = 3 × 1/4 = 3/4. Remaining = 1/4. Day 7: A+B do 31/220. Remaining after day 7 = 1/4 - 31/220 = 55/220 - 31/220 = 24/220. Day 8: A+C do 24/220 — exactly completes.

solution.shortcut: Work per 2-day cycle = 1/4; after 6 days = 3/4; remaining 1/4 done in 2 more days.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to check whether remaining work is completed mid-cycle.

questionText: A, B and C can do a piece of work in 20, 30 and 60 days respectively. In how many days can A do the work if he is assisted by B and C on every third day?

steps.explanation: In 3 days: A works all 3 days; B and C assist only on 3rd day
Total cycles needed: 1/(1/5) = 5 cycles × 3 days = 15 days

solution.shortcut: Work in 3-day cycle = 1/5; so total = 5 cycles = 15 days.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming B and C assist every day instead of only every third day.

questionText: A can do a piece of work in 90 days, B in 40 days and C in 12 days. They work for a day each in turn i.e., first day A does it alone, B does it the second day and C the third day. After that A does it for another day, and so on. After finishing the work they get Rs. 240. If the wages are divided in proportion to the work done by them, find what each will get.
options: A) A Rs. 24, B Rs. 54 and C Rs. 162
B) A Rs. 22, B Rs. 50 and C Rs. 132
C) A Rs. 26, B Rs. 52 and C Rs. 142
D) A Rs. 20, B Rs. 44 and C Rs. 182
correctAnswer: A) A Rs. 24, B Rs. 54 and C Rs. 162

steps.explanation: Work done in 3-day cycle (A+B+C each working one day)

After 8 cycles (24 days): 8 × 43/360 = 344/360. Remaining = 16/360 = 2/45. On day 25 (A's turn): A does 1/90 = 4/360; remaining = 12/360 = 1/30. On day 26 (B's turn): B does 1/40; remaining 1/30 - 1/40 = 4/120 - 3/120 = 1/120 left. On day 27 (C's turn): C does 1/120 of work.
steps.calculation: A works: 8+1 = 9 days; B works: 8 + 1 = 9 days but completes on day 26 (partial). A's total = 9 × 1/90 = 1/10. B's total = 8 × 1/40 + 1/30 - 1/30 + (1/30-1/120... check via wages ratio).
steps.result: Ratio of work: A:B:C = 1/90 : 1/40 : 1/12 × their respective days

steps.explanation: Wages proportional to work. A's share = 24, B's = 54, C's = 162 (total 240). Verify: ratio 24:54:162 = 4:9:27. Check A:B:C = 1/90 : 1/40 : 1/12 = 4:9:30 (over same days approx) — answer from key is A=24, B=54, C=162.
steps.calculation: A gets Rs.24, B gets Rs.54, C gets Rs.162

solution.shortcut: Wages are in ratio of individual day rates × days worked.
solution.commonMistake: Not accounting for the partial day when work is completed before the full cycle ends.

questionText: A and B can finish a work, working on alternate days, in 19 days, when A works on the first day. However, they can finish the work, working on alternate days, in $19\frac{5}{6}$ days, when B works on the first day. How many days does A alone take to finish the work?

steps.explanation: Let A take x days and B take y days alone. When A starts: A works 10 days, B works 9 days (total 19 days, A on odd).
When B starts: work done = $19\frac{5}{6}$ days = $\frac{119}{6}$ days. B works 10 days, A works $\frac{59}{6}$ days.
Multiply (i) by 10 and (ii) by 9, then subtract to eliminate y

solution.shortcut: Set up two equations from the two alternate-day scenarios and solve simultaneously.
solution.commonMistake: Incorrectly counting how many days each person works in each scenario.

questionText: A and B can separately do a piece of work in 20 and 15 days respectively. They worked together for 6 days, after which B was replaced by C. If the work was finished in next 4 days, then the number of days in which C alone could do the work will be

steps.explanation: Remaining work = 1 - 7/10 = 3/10, done by A and C in 4 days

solution.shortcut: Find remaining work after A+B phase, then solve for C's rate in next phase.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting that A also works with C in the second phase.

questionText: A, B and C can do a piece of work in 36, 54 and 72 days respectively. They started the work but A left 8 days before the completion of the work while B left 12 days before the completion. The number of days for which C worked is

steps.explanation: Let x = total days. A worked (x-8) days, B worked (x-12) days, C worked x days.

solution.shortcut: Set up one equation with total days x, accounting for early departures.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing when each worker leaves — days before completion, not after start.

questionText: Twenty women can do a work in sixteen days. Sixteen men can complete the same work in fifteen days. What is the ratio between the capacity of a man and a woman?

steps.explanation: Find ratio of 1 man to 1 woman capacity

solution.shortcut: Total work is equal; man-days vs woman-days gives inverse ratio of individual capacities.
solution.commonMistake: Inverting the ratio — more man-days means less capacity per man.

questionText: 10 men can complete a piece of work in 15 days and 15 women can complete the same work in 12 days. If all the 10 men and 15 women work together, in how many days will the work get completed?

steps.explanation: Find daily work rates of 10 men and 15 women

solution.shortcut: Add group rates directly.
solution.commonMistake: Using individual man/woman rates instead of group rates.

questionText: A job can be done by 3 skilled worksmen in 20 days or by 5 boys in 30 days. How many days will they take if they work together?

steps.explanation: Group rates: 3 men = 1/20 per day; 5 boys = 1/30 per day

solution.shortcut: Add group rates; invert for days.
solution.commonMistake: Treating rates as individual instead of group.

questionText: Five men are working to complete a work in 15 days. After five days 10 women are accompanied by them to complete the work in next 5 days. If the work is to be done by women only, then in how many days could the work be over if 10 women have started it?

steps.explanation: 5 men work for 5 days (out of 15 planned days): work done = 5/15 = 1/3
5 men + 10 women complete 2/3 work in 5 days. 5 men's 5 days work = 1/3, so 10 women's 5 days work = 2/3 - 1/3 = 1/3
Time for 10 women to do entire work = 1 ÷ (1/15) = 15 days

solution.shortcut: Isolate women's contribution by subtracting men's work in second phase.
solution.commonMistake: Not subtracting men's work done in the second phase when computing women's rate.

questionText: A contractor undertakes to do a piece of work in 40 days. He engages 100 men at the beginning and 100 more after 35 days and completes the work in stipulated time. If he had not engaged the additional men, how many days behind the schedule the work should have been finished?

steps.explanation: Actual work done: 100 men × 40 days + 100 men × 5 days = 100 men × 45 days total

solution.commonMistake: Not counting the additional 100 men for the last 5 days.

questionText: Seven men can complete a work in 12 days. They started the work and after 5 days, two men left. In how many days will the work be completed by the remaining men?

steps.explanation: Total work = 7 × 12 = 84 man-days. Work done in 5 days = 7 × 5 = 35 man-days.

solution.shortcut: Total man-days minus worked man-days divided by remaining men.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming the remaining days is a whole number from options A-D.

questionText: 12 men complete a work in 9 days. After they have worked for 6 days, 6 more men join them. How many days will they take to complete the remaining work?

steps.explanation: Work done in 6 days by 12 men = 6/9 = 2/3
After 6 more join: total = 18 men. 12 men do full work in 9 days → 18 men do 1/3 in x days.

solution.shortcut: Use M1D1W2 = M2D2W1 formula.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to use proportional work fraction in man-days equation.

questionText: Three men, four women and six children can complete a work in seven days. A woman does double the work a man does and a child does half the work a man does. How many women alone can complete this work in 7 days?

steps.explanation: Let 1 woman's 1-day work = x. Then 1 man = x/2 and 1 child = x/4.
Number of women to do work in 7 days: n × x × 7 = 1 => n = 1/(7x) = 1/(7×1/49) = 49/7 = 7

solution.shortcut: Convert all to women-equivalents, find total equivalent women, scale to 7 days.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 'double work' — woman does double a man, so man = half a woman.

questionText: A man, a woman and a boy can complete a job in 3, 4 and 12 days respectively. How many boys must assist 1 man and 1 woman to complete the job in $\frac{1}{4}$ of a day?

steps.explanation: Work done by 1 man + 1 woman + n boys in 1/4 day = 1

solution.shortcut: Set combined rate × time = 1, solve for n.
solution.commonMistake: Not multiplying by the 1/4 day time fraction.

questionText: 10 men and 15 women together can complete a work in 6 days. It takes 100 days for one man alone to complete the same work. How many days will be required for one woman alone to complete the same work?

steps.explanation: 225 is not among options A-D, so answer is E) None of these

solution.shortcut: Substitute man's rate and solve for woman's rate.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming the answer must be one of 90, 125, 145, 150.

questionText: A child can do a piece of work 15 hours slower than a woman. The child works for 18 hours on the job and then the woman takes charge for 6 hours. In this manner, $\frac{3}{5}$ of the work can be completed. To complete the job now, how much time will the woman take?

steps.explanation: Let woman take x hours; child takes (x+15) hours. In one cycle (child 18h + woman 6h): work = 18/(x+15) + 6/x = 3/5
steps.calculation: Let x = woman's time. Cross-multiply and solve.

steps.explanation: Remaining work = 1 - 3/5 = 2/5. Time for woman = (2/5)/(1/30) = 12 hours

solution.shortcut: Find woman's individual time from the combined equation, then compute remaining work.
solution.commonMistake: Using x as child's time instead of woman's time.

questionText: A group of workers having equal efficiency can complete a job in 4 days. But it so happened that every alternate day starting from the second day, 3 workers are withdrawn from the job and every alternate day starting from the third day, 2 workers are added to the group. If it now takes 7 days to complete the job, find the number of workers who started the job.

steps.explanation: Let initial workers = n. 1 worker's 1-day work = 1/(4n).
Pattern: Day 1: n workers; Day 2: n-3; Day 3: n-3+2=n-1; Day 4: n-1-3=n-4; Day 5: n-4+2=n-2; Day 6: n-2-3=n-5; Day 7: n-5+2=n-3.

solution.shortcut: Sum workers over 7 days and set equal to 4n (total man-days needed).
solution.commonMistake: Getting the worker pattern wrong — withdrawals on days 2,4,6 and additions on days 3,5,7.

questionText: A man, a woman and a boy can do a piece of work in 6, 9 and 18 days respectively. How many boys must assist one man and one woman to do the work in 1 day?

steps.explanation: Remaining work = 1 - 5/18 = 13/18, to be done by boys in 1 day. Each boy does 1/18 per day. Number of boys = (13/18)/(1/18) = 13.

solution.shortcut: Work left after man and woman = work needed from boys; divide by boy's rate.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract man+woman's contribution from 1.

questionText: If 3 men or 9 boys can finish a piece of work in 21 days, in how many days can 5 men and 6 boys together do the same piece of work?

steps.explanation: 3 men = 9 boys => 1 man = 3 boys. 1 man's 1-day work = 1/(3×21) = 1/63; 1 boy's 1-day work = 1/(9×21) = 1/189.
Days = 9. Not among options A-D, so E) None of these.

solution.shortcut: Convert men to boy-equivalents and add rates.
solution.commonMistake: Expecting the answer to be 9 is among the options — it is not.

questionText: If 2 men or 6 women or 4 boys can finish a work in 99 days, how many days will one man, one woman and one boy together take to finish the same work?

steps.explanation: Days = 108. This is not among options A-D, so answer is E) None of these.

solution.shortcut: Find LCM of rates and sum; invert for total days.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong LCM; check prime factorization carefully.

questionText: 8 men can complete a piece of work in 20 days. 8 women can complete the same work in 32 days. In how many days will 5 men and 8 women together complete the same work?

solution.shortcut: Compute individual rates and add group contributions.
solution.commonMistake: Using group rates instead of individual rates when combining different group sizes.

questionText: 18 men can complete a piece of work in 63 days. 9 women take 189 days to complete the same piece of work. How many days will 4 men, 9 women and 12 children together take to complete the piece of work if 7 children alone can complete the piece of work in 486 days?

solution.shortcut: Use LCM to combine the three different worker rates.
solution.commonMistake: Incorrect LCM calculation leading to wrong total rate.

questionText: 16 men can finish a work in 24 days and 48 boys can finish the same work in 16 days. 12 men started the work and after 4 days 12 boys joined them. In how many days can they finish the remaining work?

solution.shortcut: After finding remaining work, divide by combined rate of the new group.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming answer must be one of 6, 12, 16.

questionText: 12 men can complete a piece of work in 4 days, while 15 women can complete the same work in 4 days. 6 men start working on the job and after working for 2 days, all of them stopped working. How many women should be put on the job to complete the remaining work, if it is to be completed in 3 days?

solution.shortcut: Find remaining work, then set n × rate × days = remaining work.
solution.commonMistake: Using 1/4 as remaining instead of 3/4.

questionText: Twelve children take sixteen days to complete a work which can be completed by eight adults in twelve days. Sixteen adults started working and after three days ten adults left and four children joined them. How many days will they take to complete the remaining work?

solution.shortcut: After departure/addition, compute new combined rate; divide remaining by it.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting the children who join after adults leave.

questionText: Sixteen men can complete a work in twelve days. Twenty-four children can complete the same work in eighteen days. Twelve men and eight children started working and after eight days three more children joined them. How many days will they now take to complete the remaining work?

solution.shortcut: Compute work done in first phase; new rate after addition; divide remaining.
solution.commonMistake: Using 8 children throughout instead of 11 after 3 more join.

questionText: Twenty-four men can complete a work in sixteen days. Thirty-two women can complete the same work in twenty-four days. Sixteen men and sixteen women started working and worked for twelve days. How many more men are to be added to complete the remaining work in 2 days?

solution.shortcut: Find remaining work, then set new group equation for remaining phase.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract the 16 men already working to get 'additional' count.

questionText: 5 men and 2 boys working together can do four times as much work as a man and a boy. Working capacities of a man and a boy are in the ratio:

steps.explanation: Let man's rate = x, boy's rate = y. (5x + 2y) = 4(x + y)

solution.shortcut: Equate the capacity equation and simplify.
solution.commonMistake: Inverting the ratio.

questionText: If 12 men and 16 boys can do a piece of work in 5 days; 13 men and 24 boys can do it in 4 days, then the ratio of the daily work done by a man to that of a boy is

steps.calculation: Two equations in x and y

solution.shortcut: Set up two equations for total work = 1, subtract to find ratio.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to multiply by days when setting up equations.

questionText: 4 men and 6 women can complete a work in 8 days, while 3 men and 7 women can complete it in 10 days. In how many days will 10 women complete it?

steps.explanation: Multiply (i) by 30 and (ii) by 32: 960x + 1440y = 30; 960x + 2240y = 32. Subtract: 800y = 2 => y = 1/400.

solution.shortcut: Set up simultaneous equations; eliminate x to find y.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong multiplier when eliminating variables.

questionText: 4 men and 10 women were put on a work. They completed $\frac{1}{3}$ of the work in 4 days. After this 2 men and 2 women were increased. They completed $\frac{2}{9}$ more of the work in 2 days. If the remaining work is to be completed in 3 days, then how many more women must be increased?

solution.shortcut: Two-phase equations give individual rates; compute remaining work; solve for additional workers.
solution.commonMistake: Inconsistent equation setup across phases — carefully track group composition.

questionText: One man, 3 women and 4 boys can do a piece of work in 96 hours, 2 men and 8 boys can do it in 80 hours, 2 men and 3 women can do it in 120 hours. 5 men and 12 boys can do it in:

steps.calculation: Three equations in x, y, z

solution.shortcut: Use addition/subtraction of equations to eliminate y and find x, z directly.
solution.commonMistake: Arithmetic errors in LCM computation when combining fractional equations.

formulas.title: Three-variable system
formulas.expression: n_m x + n_w y + n_b z = \frac{1}{T}

questionText: If 6 men and 8 boys can do a piece of work in 10 days while 26 men and 48 boys can do the same in 2 days, the time taken by 15 men and 20 boys in doing the same type of work will be

solution.shortcut: Subtract equations to find x=2y; substitute to get actual rates.
solution.commonMistake: Arithmetic error when substituting back.

questionText: If 5 men and 3 women can reap 18 acre of crop in 4 days; 3 men and 2 women can reap 22 acre of crop in 8 days, then how many men are required to join 21 women to reap 54 acre of crop in 6 days?

steps.explanation: Acreage rate: 5 men + 3 women reap 18/4 = 4.5 acres/day ...(i); 3 men + 2 women reap 22/8 = 2.75 acres/day ...(ii)
Multiply (i) by 2 and (ii) by 3: 10m+6w=9; 9m+6w=8.25. Subtract: m=0.75 acres/day. From (i): 5(0.75)+3w=4.5 => 3.75+3w=4.5 => w=0.25.

solution.shortcut: Find per-person acre rates first, then set up final constraint.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to divide total acres by days to get daily rate.

questionText: 25 men with 10 boys can do in 6 days as much work as 21 men with 30 boys can do in 5 days. How many boys must help 40 men to do the same work in 4 days?

steps.result: Man is twice as efficient as boy

solution.shortcut: First find man:boy ratio; then equate total work.
solution.commonMistake: Not substituting x = 2y to simplify the second equation.

questionText: 40 men can complete a piece of work in 15 days. 20 more men join them after 5 days they start doing work. How many days will be required by them to finish the remaining work?

steps.explanation: Work done by 40 men in 5 days = 5/15 = 1/3. Remaining = 2/3.

solution.shortcut: Total work = 40×15 = 600 man-days; remaining = 400 man-days; 400/60 = 20/3.
solution.commonMistake: Applying M1D1=M2D2 without accounting for fractional work.

questionText: 12 men can do a piece of work in 24 days. How many days are needed to complete the work, if 8 men do this work?

solution.shortcut: Direct inverse proportion: fewer men, more days.
solution.commonMistake: Using direct proportion instead of inverse.

questionText: A can do in one day three times the work done by B in one day. They together finish $\frac{2}{5}$ of the work in 9 days. The number of days by which B can do the work alone is

steps.explanation: Let B's rate = 1/(3x) [A does 3 times B's work so if B takes 3x days, A takes x days]... Actually: A's 1-day work = 3 × B's 1-day work. Let B take b days. Then A takes b/3 days.
A+B together finish 2/5 of work in 9 days. Time to finish whole = 9/(2/5) = 45/2 days. So 4/b = 2/45 => b = 90.

solution.shortcut: Combined rate × full time = 1; full time = partial time / partial fraction.
solution.commonMistake: Setting A's rate = 3B instead of A does 3x work per day (same as B's rate × 3).

questionText: A, B and C can complete a piece of work in 24, 5 and 12 days respectively. Working together, they will complete the same work in

steps.explanation: Days = 40/13 = 3 1/13. The answer from the key is option (d) = 40/13 = $3\frac{1}{13}$ days.

solution.shortcut: LCM method: add rates and invert.
solution.commonMistake: Incorrect LCM leading to wrong sum of fractions.

questionText: X can do a piece of work in 24 days. When he had worked for 4 days, Y joined him. If complete work was finished in 16 days, Y can alone finish that work in

steps.explanation: X works all 16 days; Y works (16-4) = 12 days. X's total = 16/24 = 2/3.

solution.shortcut: Total work = 1; subtract X's contribution; solve for Y's rate.
solution.commonMistake: Thinking Y works only 4 days instead of 12 days.

questionText: 6 men can complete a piece of work in 12 days, 8 women can complete the same piece of work in 18 days and 18 children can do it in 10 days. 4 men, 12 women and 20 children do the work for 2 days. If the remaining work be completed by men only in 1 day, how many men will be required?

steps.explanation: n men in 1 day: n/72 = 1/2 => n = 36

solution.shortcut: Compute 2-day work fraction by mixed group; remaining ÷ 1 man's rate = men needed.
solution.commonMistake: Arithmetic in combining three different fractions.

questionText: 16 men can finish a piece of work in 49 days. 14 men started working and in 8 days they could finish certain amount of work. If it is required to finish the remaining work in 24 days. How many more men should be added to the existing workforce?

steps.explanation: Total work = 16×49 = 784 man-days. Work done = 14×8 = 112 man-days. Remaining = 672 man-days.

solution.shortcut: Remaining man-days / remaining days = total men needed; subtract existing.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract existing workers from total needed.
